This is the first time I have posted here.   I have been visiting the clusterbusters site off an on for the past couple of years - but I have never felt compelled to post anything here until now.  Every time I read a post where someone describes what they are going through I can relate.  Some of the stories here are really sad - and I hope these people take the busters' advice because its all good and has helped me immensely.  I have been reduced to tears reading many of the stories because I know what it is like, and I have had help and I am finally making progress and I there might be some light at the end of this very dark tunnel I have been living in for years,
I have been dealing with CH now for 13 years.  For the first couple of years I was told that I had a a severe case of Sinusitis and that was what was causing my pain.  The doc would give me some Avamys or some other nasal cortosoid, some antibiotics and send me on my way.  This 'worked' for a while as my clusters only lasted for a couple of  weeks and I figured that the sinus infection had passed and I was fine again.  This happened two to three times a year.  No biggie.   The attacks became worse and less tolerable as time went on.   I started to suspect that I did not have sinusitis - even though I felt like I had been kicked in the face and my sinuses were plugged.  At the time I did not have a regular doctor and if I showed up at the hospital or urgent care facility suffering from a CH I was often dismissed as 'drug seeking' and sent home, because the time that any doctor decided to see me the CH had passed and I was feeling 'normal' again.  I did eventually find a doctor who diagnosed me with CH, but he did not know too much about it and treated it like a standard migraine.   Maxalt as an abortive and amytripaline as a preventative - that didn't work, but at least I had a name for what I was dealing with now.  10 years ago the internet was not what it is now.  Cluster Headaches were less known and there were virtually no options for people.  My CH was episodic for 8 years - then they starting getting worse.  The cycles got longer and more frequent, until I got to the point where I am now.   Chronic CH - 4-6 times a day for the past  five years.  I get my 'breaks' from time to time where I will experience a one or two days CH free and I have good days (1 or 2) and I have really bad days (6+ attacks per day).
About three years ago I was fortunate enough to be put in touch with the CHAMP (Calgary Headache Assessment and Management Program) in Calgary.   The team of docs there are nothing short of amazing.  I have had MRI's done, I got a prescription for oxygen (a bit of a struggle to get initially from GP - CHAMP said NO PROBLEM), I got a scrip of Sumitriptan (Immitrex) and they started trying out different preventative meds.   I tried Verapimil.   It didn't really help me, I kept going for my ECG's and all was well until I hit the effective dosage and then BAM the worst tension head

On 3/13/2026 at 5:02 PM, Nikkk said:
I read often about people talking about busting, saying that in order to bust with mushrooms you need to feel the high. While I understand that micro dose don't seem to be very effective in terms of busting for most; do we really need to feel the psychotropic effects in order to bust?
Technically, getting high and having a therapeutic result are unrelated.  The "trip" has no therapeutic benefit related to CH; it's just a side effect of the medicine.  (Psychotropic seeds that are used to treat CH usually have no trip associated with them.)  However, with mushrooms there is a fairly strong likelihood that if you don't have some kind of trip, you haven't taken enough to treat the CH. A trip level is recommended for that reason.  In the earliest days of busting, a lot was made of the fact that people could take MM at "subhallucinogenic" levels and still treat their CH.  This might still be true, but a trip-level dose is generally recommended here for the reason I gave -- to make it more sure that you are taking enough.
Don't get me or Jeebs started on BOL, or 2-bromo-lsd.  We lived through the days when it did not seem impossible that it might become available to people with CH.  A company named Entheogen (
Entheogen Pharmaceuticals – Supplier of Psychoactive Substances Derived from Plants And Fungi
) temporarily had an agreement with Harvard University, which owns the patent on BOL-148/2-bromo-LSD, to license the rights to making it if it could pass clinical trials.  The company raised money from people with CH and others for those trials (very expensive!!), and hope was in the air for a few years, but they never raised enough $ and that was the end of it. (There was lots of other drama, as when a friend of ClusterBusters, a Harvard med school professor, took out a patent (along with some other people) on the use of BOL to treat CH, which added complications -- and they excluded another friend of CB, a Yale professor, from that arrangement. That's more or less the story, which is available in juicier and unhappier detail on the internet for anyone who wants to look for it.)

text:
If BOL-148 becomes available, that would have to be noticed for migraine big time, right?
Just a note about this (which is more pedantry than enlightenment, but might be interesting to some). BOL was actually tested for migraine quite a few years ago (1963), and seems (maybe) to have been shown to be less effective for migraine than for CH.Â  The test was apparently not very well or carefully done.
In a long and testy thread in the comments section here --
http://www.dosenation.com/listing.php?id=7955
-- Jon Hanna, a longtime advocate of psychedelics, says:
>> Sicuteri found LSD to be more effective than BOL-148 for treating migraine, and he found methysergide to be more effective than LSD. Sicuteri's less-promising results using BOL-148 for treating migraine could explain Halpern and Passie's "surprise" that BOL-148 "seems even more effective than LSD" for treating cluster headaches; but, of course, migraines and cluster headaches are not the same.<<
and Dr. Sewell [RIP] says (actually at an earlier point in the comments)
>>>I would say that Jon Hanna is correct, and that the idea was inspired by Frederigo Sicuteri's 1963 paper "Prophylactic Treatment of Migraine by Means of Lysergic Acid Derivatives". In it, Dr. Sicuteri gave LSD-25, BOL-148, and methysergide to 238 patients with a mixture of headache types--cluster, migraine, what sounds like tension headache, and "chronic intracranial inflammation" headache, whatever that is. He found that methysergide worked better than LSD, which worked better than BOL.
What he didn't do, however, was separate out the cluster headache group and ask how well LSD worked to terminate their cluster periods or extend their remission periods. He came very close to discovering LSD's therapeutic properties for cluster headache, fifty years ago, but just didn't ask the right questions.<<<
This doesn't say that BOL might not be the very best medicine available for migraine, given the side-effects (and unavailability in many places) of methysergide and the illegality of LSD; just saying maybe it's not as likely to be effective for migraine as it seems to be for CH.
(Incidentally, a few years ago, I suggested here that Dr. Sewell had had a falling out with Dr. Halpern over the commercialization of BOL. I was roundly criticized for having said that, but I think anyone who decides to read all the comments will understand why I came to that conclusion.Â  Maybe they patched it up later, or maybe I drew the wrong conclusion in the first place.Â  Not important now, really . . . I guess I just don't like being wrong.)

We did our science homework, contacted doctors and researchers and anyone with any information on how to improve the efficacy of psychedelic treatments and make sure that everyone was as safe as possible.

My presentation called Psychedelics or Suicide was heartfelt and true. Many people have made that decision and have stepped back from the edge.
We have changed legislation. We have gotten the first pharmaceutical company in history to run a phase three study on a promising new treatment.
We have the top headache specialists in the world attending our conferences.

Even though they both contain LSA, HBWR is really a whole different animal than RC.  It is extremely rare (about 1 in 50, I would say) for anyone to experience significant nausea with RC.  A lot depends on how the HBWR is prepared -- the hull definitely can cause the kinds of symptoms you describe.  But you can take subpsychedelic doses of RC, with no preparation hassles (just crush, soak, and drink) and still have very good impact on your CH.
Maybe I'm wrong about this, but my thought about your MM approach is that if the receptors are closed to trippiness and related sensations, they're also going to be closed to the therapeutic effects.  The two are kind of inseparable, I think.  Not completely inseparable -- we have seen people treat CH successfully with MM doses small enough to create only very minimal trip/nausea effects -- but at the higher doses most often recommended here, the medicinal value is accompanied by at least some level of psychedelic effect.
Bromo (BOL-148) treats clusters without psychedelic effect because the hallucinogenic properties of what would otherwise be a massive dose of LSD are eliminated by the addition of a bromine atom (or molecule, or something).  There's really no comparison to what you're considering.
I guess the key thing here, to be clear, is that it's not the tripping that treats the CH; it's the substance (the psilocin, the LSA, the LSD, the 5-MEO-Dalt, whatever).  When tripping occurs it's just a side effect, often unwanted.  In my undereducated opinion, if the receptors are closed to trippiness etc., they're also gonna be closed to the medicine part.
Another MM alternative for you would seem to be to take a "small" dose that's as large as you're willing to tolerate, and see what happens over the next five days. In the early days of busting, the recommended first dose was as low as .25 gram.  That was partly to test tolerance, but in some cases that amount, or amounts as "small" as .5 gram, worked to treat the CH.  Since you're having some issues with .1, maybe there is no real MM dose small enough not to affect you but large enough to deal with your CH.
But maybe what you're trying will work, or is working.
Wish we had answers instead of what are essentially questions and theories.

An Herbal Protocol for the Treatment of Cluster Headache
Leslie J Genser
Summary
Using tincture of Licorice root as the principal therapeutic agent and tincture of Skullcap as a rehabilitative and sedative nervine, cluster headache can be gently and effectively treated and sufferers restored to normal functioning faster and more economically than possible using chemical pharmaceuticals, without damaging side effects.
Introduction
They are known as 'Suicide headaches' as they have driven many women and men to seek a permanent end to unbearable suffering. They are considered by many authorities to be among the most intensely painful things a human can experience; yet they leave no mark of passage, no damage to the body and few clues to their origin. And modern medicine is virtually unable to stop them.
Unless you suffer from them yourself, or know someone who does, you are in all likelihood unfamiliar with cluster headaches. This includes most physicians, and given the relatively low incidence of the syndrome this is not surprising. Cluster headache or CH is thought to affect approximately .07% of the population, about 7 per thousand people,  with men affected 6 times more than women.  Many practitioners can go years without seeing a cluster patient, and when and if they do both the body of knowledge and treatment options available to them are inadequate, ineffective and in some cases actually harmful. I will touch on this in more detail later on, but first we must consider what the cluster headache syndrome is and how it affects people.
I must begin with a brief biographical note: I have suffered from cluster headaches for roughly forty years, at times episodic and at others chronic (I will explain the distinction in the next section). I have been misdiagnosed, treated for the wrong condition, prescribed ridiculously dangerous and addictive drugs, told I was imagining things, and told to go away. This is sadly a fairly common experience among cluster sufferers, especially older ones. I wish to stress this point because unlike the stereotypical male obstetrician, I know precisely what it's like to have this baby. I began this research because I was desperate for a normal life, and I was no longer willing to accept inadequate care or quiet suffering as my only options. I had an enormous advantage from the start: A willing test subject who worked cheap, was always available and provided instant feedback. At that time I started this project I was experiencing an average of three to a maximum of six moderate to severe episodes a day, and an additional three (usually far worse) at night. There was no shortage of raw material to work with.
Definitions

SO HOW WELL DOES THIS WORK?
When the tryptamine method works well, especially when it is first tried, the relief seems miraculous. Clusterheads who have tried everything with limited or no success are amazed at the results, and they tend to praise mushrooms or seeds or LSD to the skies. It does work miracles for some - there are true tales of a dose or two ending a cycle instantly.
The reality for most is that it works very well, but the process is more like work than a miracle. There will be setbacks, there will be attacks to endure while refraining from old standbys like Imitrex. Many will need more than just a few doses of tryptamines, and some will have to use the treatment every one or two weeks for long periods.
But most will enjoy the most effective treatment they’ve known. How many is most? What are the statistics on the effectiveness of tryptamines? There is little good, solid, publishable, scientific data. But the Clusterbusters feel confidant that at least two-thirds and maybe as many as 90 percent of clusterheads can find significant relief using tryptamines.
There has been a case review study, and while such a study is not strong proof, it indicates the method is good enough to warrant further study. There is also a small clinical trial - again not the most powerful of research, but quite valid, showing very good results with a non-hallucinogenic tryptamine called BOL 148.
There are also collections of some hundreds of anecdotal reports from clusterheads themselves - which the Clusterbusters called the Shroom Stories and the Seed Stories. They are hardly scientific evidence, but the numbers have been consistent over many years as the collections have grown, and the results are not inconsistent with the case review study.
THE CASE REVIEW STUDY
Two researchers at McLean Hospital in Boston conducted a case review study of 56 clusterheads who took part in a survey on their experiences using tryptamines. They reported that 52 percent using psilocybin stopped an ongoing cycle entirely, and another 37 percent found it partially effective. Only 6 percent had no success at all.
For extending the pain-free time between cycles, tryptamines were even more effective. Of the 24 clusterheads who tried it, 89 percent found psilocybin or LSD effective. For using a small dose of psilocybin or LSD to stop an individual attack, 82 percent found success.
A case review study is not definitive scientific proof of effectiveness. Solid, scientific proof of a medical treatment would require a randomized, double-blind clinical trial, ideally involving hundreds of people. Such studies can cost many millions of dollars, even if regulators and research review committees would allow such a study involving illegal substances.

NEUROLOGY 2006;66:1920Â–1922
Response of cluster headache to psilocybin and LSD
R. Andrew Sewell, John H. Halpern and Harrison G. Pope, Jr.

Hi,
I apologize if this sounds like a ramble - I've been on topamax, but have decided to quit taking it. I can't stand it, it turns my brain to pudding.
I just discovered this site after decades of migraines and what I believe to be clusters. Only recently have I had a GP who'd even listen to me, but never the less, I'm starting to reach the end of my rope. I'm clearly not suffering as bad as many of you, but I'm just so worn down.Â  He at least prescribed Fioricet and Imitrex, which for the most part, were god sends.
I'm 41 and have had migraines since I was 9. Back then, they were classic migraines w/o aura. They'd come and go in clusters - I'd go years w/o having them. But the symptoms were classic - vomiting, unilateral pain, photo and phonophobia. In my late 30s, they seemed to start evolving, presenting symptoms of both migraines AND clusters at times. I rarely have classic photo/phonophobia anymore, but the stomach symptoms still hit me on occasion when I have a daytime headache.
But sometimes I'll wake up around 2-3 in the morning with raging pain, and I know I'm screwed. No amount of Imitrex or Fioricet works and I physically can't stand to lie down. It turns into a 4-5 hour long festival of pacing, vomiting, watering eyes, runny nose, ice pick to my eye, back of the head, throbbing, just on the edge of screaming and generally wishing I could knock myself unconscious. Symptoms of both migraine AND clusters.
The last bout I had like that prompted me to *finally* schedule an appointment with a migraine specialist/neurologist. I went in with high hopes, expecting to explain my symptoms, work through a course of treatment, but no, that didn't happen. He flatly told me there's no such thing as a mixture of cluster and migraine symptoms and sent me on my way with a prescription for Topamax. The final straw was when I asked, "Does Topamax have any cognitive effects?", he flatly said, "No".
I've tried the Topamax for about a week, but I simply can't stand it. I'm only at 25mg, 2x a day and I feel like a bloody moron. I'm an engineer by trade and there's no way I can "wait a month to push through the symptoms" - I'm a zombie all day at work, fading in and out, having a hard time even reading at times. The only reason I'm able to write this is because I haven't taken my evening dose and I'm not going to. Needless to say, that neurologist is an ass in my book who is now fired.
Luckily, the full on cluster (like) headaches aren't super common, but it seems like I'm always right on the edge of a migraine at this point. But I'm in constant fear of another headache like that last night-time hell.Â  I had moderate success experimenting with racetams in the past (reducing trigger sensitivity just a bit). Reading a recent article about clusters and 2-bromo-LSD tripped a long lost memory - when I was in my 20s, I experimented with extracting LSA from Morning Glories for recreational purposes. It occurred to me that I had few or no migraines back then. Furt

apologizing for not meeting my mission
. I wanted to get everyone access to BOL-148, also known as 2-Bromo-LSD. This compound has had an interesting history- it was synthesized by Albert Hoffman who 'discovered' LSD and was used as a radiolabeling compound in some experiments decades ago. As we know, some serotonergic psychedelics may help with cluster headaches (psilocybin)(1)
The idea was that, since BOL-148 basically acts as a non-psychedelic neutral agonist at many of the sites of LSD(2), it could provide a safe & effective therapeutic effect for those who have responded well to a psychedelic but want something that won't alter mental status. It could possibly be used as a preventative measure to abate cluster headaches (3).
Since I embarked upon the journey of 'developing' BOL, I ended up in a biopharmaceutical adventure that gave me the privilege of starting/growing a few companies, one of which has a drug in the clinic.

The German 2-Bromo-LSD (BOL-148) study:
https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/c050/a80fb0d48843cc1e2b6af9f5ea497632137c.pdf
Sewell, Halpern, and Pope on LSD and Psilocybin:
https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/f49e/8e93235ed5945ade9a38323a1233ea0aa5f5.pdf
This 2006 study is interesting for a lot of reasons. For one, you can see that a very large percentage of people report success with psilo and acid as
abortives
, but that isn't even anything we really talk about here anymore, at least in part because psilo at least hasn't been a very good abortive for people. Another thing is that no "protocol" is addressed: no detox from triptans, no five-day wait.  It could be that people were following the protocol, but it could also be that they were not. Other things: (1) the relatively low reported efficacy of oxygen, which we can probably attribute to low flows or other systemic factors; (2) the fact that the authors talk about these substances being used successfully at "subhallucinogenic" levels, which in general is argued here to be likely to be ineffective; (3) the observation that one dose of LSD was often sufficient to end a cycle, and rarely were more than three doses of psilo needed.  Don't ask me what it all means.  Sewell's LSA "research," also conducted by interviews/questionnaires, has similarly perplexing qualities.
